title: Fixing Type Inference for Overlapping Type Guards in a Generic Function

statement.markdown: Implement a generic function `isSubset<T, U>()` that takes two type parameters `T` and `U`. The function should return a boolean indicating whether `T` is a subset of `U`. If `T` and `U` are the same type, return `true`. If `T` is not a subset of `U`, return `false`. For example, `isSubset<string | number, string | number>` should return `true`, while `isSubset<string, number>` should return `false`. Note that type guards and conditional types are used in the implementation. The implementation should work for any two types `T` and `U`.
statement.inputFormat: No input is provided, only type parameters `T` and `U` are used.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a boolean value.
statement.notes: Use type guards and conditional types to solve this problem.

constraints.description: No constraints on n, this is just a placeholder.

examples.explanation: isSubset<string | number, string | number> should return true

hints.text: Use the built-in `intersection` type to check if two types have common elements
Use the `type` keyword to define a type alias for the intersection type
Use the `extends` keyword to check if one type is a subset of another

editorial.title: Editorial Explanation
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to use the `intersection` type to check if two types have common elements. We can then use the `extends` keyword to check if one type is a subset of another.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to manually check every element of type T and see if it is also an element of type U. However, this approach is not scalable and would not work for complex types.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use the `intersection` type to check if two types have common elements. We can then use the `extends` keyword to check if one type is a subset of another.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `intersection` type to check if two types have common elements, and then use the `extends` keyword to check if one type is a subset of another. This solution is scalable and works for complex types.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: To prove the correctness of this solution, we need to show that it returns the correct result for every possible input. This can be done using a formal proof or by testing the solution with many different inputs.
